FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study participants; 3498 U.S. adolescents aged 9-11 years from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as hours/day (parent-reported) and sleep duration reported. Screen time alone (B5) without qualifying smartphone/social media variable is not met. Sleep is not a qualifying exposure per B5. Paper examines sleep + screen time, but neither qualifies: screen time without smartphone/SM platform specificity = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: While Cox proportional hazards models are used, FT-IC2 is NOT_MET, making this moot for inclusion

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values present but FT-IC2 is NOT_MET, making this moot

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data with Cox proportional hazards models examining prospective associations

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplication; appears to be original publication (2025)

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; criterion does not apply because paper lacks qualifying smartphone/social media exposure
notes: EXCLUDE. Screen time without device/platform specificity and sleep duration do not meet FT-IC2. Per B5, unspecified screen time is NOT_MET

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 7445 participants aged 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (hours/day) reported but without device/platform specificity. Sleep disturbance and sleep duration are not qualifying exposures. Per B5, unspecified screen time without smartphone/app/platform breakdown = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: Weighted multivariable linear regression models used but FT-IC2 NOT_MET makes this moot for inclusion

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values present but FT-IC2 NOT_MET makes this moot

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data with weighted multivariable linear regression examining prospective associations over 2-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplication; original 2025 publication

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; criterion not applicable due to lack of qualifying smartphone/social media exposure
notes: EXCLUDE. Screen time without platform/device specificity and sleep measures do not meet FT-IC2. Per B5, unspecified screen time is NOT_MET

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 791 ABCD Study participants (Mage=14.12, 53% female) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone application use (daytime/evening 6:00AM-7:59PM, late-evening 8:00PM-9:59PM, late-night 10:00PM-5:59AM) measured via EARS application. Qualifies as app use per qualifying smartphone definition. Per B1, separate coefficients reported for app use by time-of-day categories

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models with standardized regression coefficients (standardized beta=-0.21 to 0.07, P<.001) examining associations between smartphone app use and sleep/physical activity. Meets mixed-effects coefficient criterion

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized beta coefficients reported: -0.21 to 0.07 (daytime/evening), -0.05 to 0.02 (late-evening), -0.16 to 0.27 (late-night), all with P<.001. Meets extractable numeric criterion

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data with mixed-effects models examining smartphone app use relationships with sleep and physical activity

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplication; original 2025 publication

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone (app use) is independent variable predicting outcomes (sleep, physical activity). FT-EC7 requires smartphone/SM as dependent variable with demographic predictors only. This is SM as IV predicting non-SM outcomes, excluded per section 3.1(a)
notes: INCLUDE. Meets all FT-IC1-6 criteria. Qualifying smartphone exposure (app use via EARS) with separate time-of-day coefficients. Original ABCD analysis with extractable mixed-effects regression coefficients. FT-EC7 does not apply
